params.name: restitution
params.label: Restitution (e)
params.pedagogical_note: e = 1 is perfectly elastic (KE conserved); e = 0 is perfectly inelastic (maximum KE lost); intermediate values give partial energy loss

params.name: mass_ratio
params.label: Mass Ratio (m₁/m₂)
params.pedagogical_note: At ratio = 1 and e = 1, the moving ball stops completely — a classic billiard ball result. At ratio < 1, the lighter ball bounces back.

governing_equations: p = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 = \text{const}  \quad \text{(momentum always conserved)}
v_1' = \frac{m_1 - e m_2}{m_1 + m_2} v_1  \quad \text{(when } v_2 = 0\text{)}
v_2' = \frac{(1 + e) m_1}{m_1 + m_2} v_1  \quad \text{(when } v_2 = 0\text{)}

socratic_plan.question: Both balls have the same mass and we start with a perfectly elastic collision — e = 1. When the blue ball hits the orange one, where does the blue ball end up? Does it keep moving forward, stop completely, or bounce back?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student externalises their prior model of momentum transfer before observing it
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student makes a prediction — any answer accepted

socratic_plan.question: Now try changing e. Set it to 0.5, then 0. Launch each time and describe what changes after the collision.
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student discovers that reducing e decreases how much energy is transferred — the balls move together rather than one stopping
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student notices the balls behave differently at e = 0 vs e = 1

socratic_plan.question: Look at the momentum reading — it shows what fraction is conserved after each collision. What pattern do you notice across all the different e values you just tried?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student identifies that total momentum is conserved regardless of restitution, even though kinetic energy is not
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student recognises that momentum is always ~100% conserved regardless of e

socratic_plan.question: Set e back to 1. Now change the mass ratio to 0.25 — the blue ball is four times lighter than the orange. What do you think will happen to the blue ball after the collision?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student forms a hypothesis about what happens when a lighter object hits a heavier stationary one
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student makes a prediction — particularly whether the blue ball bounces back

socratic_plan.question: The lighter ball bounced back. Using the momentum equation, why must a lighter ball reverse direction when it hits a much heavier stationary object — even in a perfectly elastic collision?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student derives from p = const and the restitution equation that v₁' must be negative when m₁ < m₂ and e = 1
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student connects the sign of v₁' to the mass ratio in the collision equations

annotate.text: v₁' = (m₁ − m₂) v₁ / (m₁ + m₂) < 0 when m₁ < m₂

verification.summary: Momentum is conserved for all restitution values. KE is fully conserved only for e = 1. At e = 0 with equal masses, exactly 50% of KE is lost. A lighter elastic ball bounces back; equal-mass elastic ball stops.

probes.description: Perfectly elastic collision between equal masses — blue ball should stop, orange takes full speed
Perfectly inelastic collision between equal masses — should retain exactly 50% KE
Elastic collision with heavy blue ball (mr = 4) — blue continues forward, orange flies off faster
Elastic collision with light blue ball (mr = 0.25) — blue bounces back at −0.6 v₀

invariants.description: KE fraction should be ≈ 1.0 for perfectly elastic equal-mass collision.
Perfectly inelastic equal-mass collision retains exactly 50% of KE.
Orange ball should receive all of blue's speed in elastic equal-mass case (v₂' = 1.0 v₀).
KE fraction should be ≈ 1.0 for elastic heavy-ball collision.
Higher restitution should yield higher KE fraction (inelastic_equal < elastic_equal).
Light blue ball (mr = 0.25, e = 1) should reverse direction at −0.6 v₀.
